free_text: Spallation neutron production by 0.8, 1.2, and 1.6 GeV
protons on Pb targets
Neutron multiplicity for natPb
are revised by authors in this reference
DPTA/SPN
National laboratory SATURNE
DAPNIA/SPhN.
Institute of nuclear physics, Orsay
Interactions Ion-Matiere
-  By authors.  Spallation neutron production in proton
induced reactions on Pb targets at 0.8, 1.2, and 1.6
GeV has been measured at the SATURNE accelerator.
Double-differential cross sections were obtained over a
broad angular range from which averaged neutron
multiplicities per reaction were inferred for energies
above 2 MeV.  The results are compared with
calculations performed with a high energy transport
code including two different intranuclear cascade
models: it is shown that the Cugnon INC model gives a
better agreement with the data than the Bertini one,
mainly because of improved nucleon - nucleon cross
sections and Pauli blocking treatment.
For low energy neutrons, < 400 MeV.
2-cm-thick lead target
For low energy neutrons, < 400 MeV
For neutron-gamma discrimination.
High energy measurements, >200 MeV, were done using
(n,p) scattering on a liquid hydrogen converter and
reconstruction of the proton trajectory in a rotating
magnetic spectrometer.
Experimental techniques, described in details.
Data tables from Mark A. Kellett
New reference and new data for sub003 were
added, according N.Otsuka comments.
All data were rounded for five decimal places
by compiler.
Half size of letargy bin.
Error bars on the results presented here take
into account only statistical uncertainties except at
the very low energies where the increase of
uncrertainty assosiated with the proximity of the
detector threshold is added.
Systematic errors in the time-of-flight method come
mainly from the subtraction of chance coincidence,
gamma rejection, and efficiency determination. They are
of the order of 10% except at high energies where the
uncertainty on the D+Be cross section makes it poorer
but always less than 15%.
Systematic errors in second method arise mainly from
the beam calibration and the unfolding procedure: they
are less than 15% at 800 and 1200 MeV but can reach 25%
at 1600 MeV because of the increasing inelastic
contribution.
*By compiler*.MONIT-REF is B.C. Barashenkov "Cross
Sections of Interactions of Particle and Nuclei
with Nuclei, JINR, Dubna, 1993.
. No information on source of uncertainties
Table V,VI,VII of J,PR,C,65,044621,2002
